Long ago, before the mountains had names, the Wind was lonely. It rushed through the valleys and over the seas, but nothing could keep pace with it. Envious of the stars, which danced together in clusters, the Wind begged the Sky for a companion. The Sky, pitying the restless gale, tore a piece of silver from the edge of a cloud and breathed life into it, creating the Echo.

The Echo was swift and formless, just like the Wind. For an age, they were inseparable. They raced through canyons, playing tag among the pines. But the Wind grew boastful, claiming it was the fastest thing in creation. To prove it, the Wind challenged the Echo to race around the world.

The Wind howled and sprinted, stripping leaves from trees in its haste. But no matter how fast it flew, the moment it stopped to catch its breath, it heard the Echo arriving right behind it, mimicking its own triumphant roar. Furious that it could not outrun its shadow, the Wind shrieked in anger and scattered in all directions.

To this day, the Wind wanders the earth in ragged gusts, forever searching for silence, while the loyal Echo follows just a step behind, repeating the Wind’s lonely song.

The Mountain despised its own permanence; it yearned to detach from its foundations and drift among the stars.

One twilight, the Mountain gathered the lightest snow from its peak and the finest mists from its valleys. It sculpted them into vast, billowing wings, attaching them to its rocky flanks with veins of silver ore. It prayed to the Wind to lift it, to grant it the power of levitation. But stone cannot fly simply by wishing; the Gravity, offended by this arrogance, pulled down with crushing force.

The wings did not lift the mountain; instead, the pressure compacted the snow and mist into something dense and unyielding. The wings shattered and fell, transforming into sleek, feathered bodies as they tumbled. They became the Falcons. Now, they soar around the peaks, diving with the speed of falling stones, forever mocking the Mountain that remains chained to the earth, dreaming of a flight it can only watch.

To capture the Thunder’s voice, the Oak commanded its sap to boil and its bark to harden into a resonant shell.
